title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sociologist called Noah Thorne talking about the evolution and impact of urban legends. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sociologist called Noah Thorne talking about the evolution and impact of urban legends. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Noah Thorne explores why certain modern myths survive and how they reflect societal anxieties.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Noah. We’ve all heard them—the story of the vanishing hitchhiker or the mysterious creature in the sewers. These are urban legends. As a sociologist, I’m fascinated by why these stories survive even when they are clearly false. My interest in folklore didn't start with a book; it actually began when I heard a [7]scary campfire story[/7] during a school trip when I was ten. I was terrified, but I also noticed how the story changed slightly every time it was retold. I wanted to know why we are so driven to share these myths.

An urban legend is more than just a lie. It’s a story that feels plausible because it’s set in the modern world. I’ve found that the [8]emotional core[/8] of a legend is the most important factor in its survival. If a story triggers strong fear or disgust, it’s much more likely to be passed on. We are biologically wired to share information about potential threats, even if that information is incorrect. It’s a survival mechanism that has gone wrong in the modern age.

Most legends follow a specific structure. They usually happen to a 'friend of a friend', which provides a thin layer of credibility without requiring a direct witness. I’ve noticed that [9]local details[/9] are often added to make the story feel more relevant. If you hear a legend in London, it might happen on the Underground; in New York, it happens in Central Park. By grounding the myth in a familiar location, the teller makes the threat feel immediate and real.

In the past, these stories spread through word of mouth or photocopied letters. Today, they move at lightning speed through social media. I’ve been researching what we call 'creepypastas'—internet-born urban legends. I found that the [10]visual evidence[/10], such as blurry photos or edited videos, is what makes these modern myths so convincing to young people. Even when we know an image can be faked, there is a part of our brain that still believes what it sees.

Urban legends often reflect the anxieties of the time. In the 1980s, many stories focused on the dangers of new technology or shifting social values. Today, I’ve noticed a rise in legends about [11]data privacy[/11] and government surveillance. These stories act as a kind of collective nightmare, allowing us to express our fears about things we cannot control. They are a mirror of our cultural subconscious.

However, these stories can have real-world consequences. I recently studied a case where a legend about a haunted house led to a significant [12]decline in property value[/12] for an entire neighborhood. People were so convinced the story was true that they refused to move there, even after the police proved nothing had ever happened. It shows that the power of a story can be stronger than the power of a fact.

Looking forward, I’m interested in how we can use the structure of urban legends for good. I believe that [13]educational campaigns[/13] could be more effective if they were told as stories rather than lists of facts. If we can make a safety message feel as 'sticky' as an urban legend, we might be able to save lives. It’s about understanding the mechanics of how we communicate.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]blog series[/14] for the University website where I debunk the most common modern myths. I want to help people develop the critical thinking skills they need to navigate the digital world. We need to learn how to spot a legend before we press 'share'. Thank you.
